label: The Exodus
description: The route the book of Exodus describes the Israelites taking out of slavery in Egypt, through the wilderness, to the edge of Canaan.

waypoints.note: Starting point: the Israelites, enslaved in Egypt, prepare to leave under Moses's leadership.
Exodus describes the Israelites crossing a body of water on dry ground as Pharaoh's army pursues them; the exact location is debated among scholars.
Moses receives the Ten Commandments and the covenant law at the mountain, per the biblical account.
The Israelites camp for much of their 40 years of wilderness wandering, including a failed initial attempt to enter Canaan.
After a generation passes, the Israelites cross the Jordan into Canaan under Joshua's leadership.

label: The Life of Jesus
description: Key locations from the Gospel accounts of Jesus's birth, upbringing, ministry, and death.

waypoints.note: Jesus is born in Bethlehem, according to Matthew and Luke, during the reign of Herod the Great.
Matthew describes Jesus's family briefly fleeing to Egypt as infants to escape Herod's violence.
Jesus grows up in this modest Galilean village, largely outside the Gospel narratives until adulthood.
Much of Jesus's early public teaching and many of his recruited disciples are centered around this lake.
Jesus is baptized by John, an event described in all four Gospels as marking the start of his public ministry.
Jesus's later ministry, crucifixion, and, per the Gospels, resurrection take place in and around this city.

label: Paul's Missionary Journeys
description: A broad narrative sequence of places associated with Paul's life and travels as described in Acts and his letters, not a precise year-by-year itinerary.

waypoints.note: Paul's hometown in Cilicia, a Roman provincial capital in Asia Minor.
The Syrian city that served as the launching point for Paul's missionary journeys and where believers were first called 'Christians.'
The site of Paul's dramatic conversion experience described in Acts, while he was traveling to persecute Christians there.
A major Asia Minor city where Paul spent an extended period establishing a large Christian community.
A Roman colony in Macedonia, traditionally regarded as the site of the first Christian community in Europe.
A wealthy Greek port city where Paul founded a church and later wrote several letters addressing internal disputes.
The imperial capital where Paul's letters and later imprisonment mark a culmination of his ministry recorded in the New Testament.
